front: War: American invasions of Canada were repelled by Tecumseh and British/Canadian forces
Event: partly caused by the British Orders in Council restricting neutral trade
Event: a raid on Prophetstown (the Battle of Tippecanoe) pushed Tecumseh's confederacy to ally with the British before this war
Event: the Chesapeake-Leopard Affair, involving the impressment of American sailors, was a direct cause
Event: Jefferson's 1807 Embargo Act, limiting trade with France and Britain, preceded this war
Battle: forces under Roger Hale Sheaffe defeated Stephen Van Rensselaer's army on the Niagara frontier
Battle: Winfield Scott and William Wadsworth fought on the American side of this battle
Battle: British general Isaac Brock was killed here
Event: the modern Citadelle of Quebec was constructed following this war
Battle: an October 1812 British victory that thwarted an American attempt to establish a base on the Canadian side of the Niagara River
Event: Laura Secord walked twenty miles to warn James FitzGibbon of an impending British ambush
Figure: rushed to help her brother James at the Battle of Queenston Heights before her famous 20-mile walk
Battle: an American attack here failed because the British had been warned by Laura Secord
Event: Isaac Brock captured Fort Detroit with help from Tecumseh, after William Hull's failed invasion of Canada
Figure: his surrender of Fort Detroit led to his court-martial
Figure: claimed to have personally killed Tecumseh at the Battle of the Thames
Event: Thomas Van Horne's forces were ambushed at Sindathon's Village; general Adam Muir then solidified his rout at the Battle of Brownstown in the ensuing Battle of the Oakwoods
Event: Duncan McArthur's raid at the Battle of Malcolm's Mills ended a theatre of this war that also included the siege of Prairie du Chien
Event: the Potawatomi won the Battle of Fort Dearborn during this war
Event: the British took the trading post of Fort Mackinac
Battle: Oliver Hazard Perry's victory was announced with the note "we have met the enemy and they are ours"
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Figure: shifted his flagship under fire from the USS Lawrence to the undamaged USS Niagara at the Battle of Lake Erie
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Figure: a free black sailor who likely repeatedly saved Oliver Hazard Perry's life during a critical maneuver at the Battle of Lake Erie
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Event: the USS Constitution defeated HMS Guerriere, as James Dacre lost the duel to Isaac Hull
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Battle: nicknamed the "False Nile" for the tactics used against the Confiance's squadron
back: Battle of Plattsburgh / Lake Champlain (War of 1812)
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Battle: the winning side hauled the Saratoga's undamaged port side around with cables
back: Battle of Plattsburgh / Lake Champlain (War of 1812)
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Figure: his scheme to attack Pacific commerce mainly entangled him in the local politics of Nuku Hiva
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Event: over 1,100 letters of marque were issued to privateers; the Young Teazer terrorized Mahone Bay
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Event: the Blue Lights were sympathizers who warned the enemy about American blockade runners
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Figure: recorded his time in the Chesapeake Bay Flotilla, which fought at the Battle of Bladensburg
work: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers

front: Event: led by British general Robert Ross, occurring shortly after his forces won the Battle of Bladensburg
Figure: ordered the Lansdowne portrait of George Washington rescued before fleeing the White House
Event: ended by a freak tornado; Paul Jennings wrote a memoir recalling it
Event: refugees sought shelter in Brookeville for a day before moving to the Octagon House
Event: a man reportedly jumped in front of a cannon shouting "Are you Goths or Vandals?" to save the Patent Office
Figure: reportedly ordered the destruction of all the letter "C" type in a newspaper building so his name could no longer be defamed in print
Figure: tried to recruit enslaved people from the American side by promising them freedom
Figure: wrote "The Star-Spangled Banner" after witnessing the bombing/siege of Fort McHenry
Work: later set to the tune of "Anacreon in Heaven"
Figure: a fugitive slave who defended Fort McHenry alongside Francis Scott Key
Figure: won the Battle of New Orleans in 1815, after the Treaty of Ghent had already ended the war
Figure: this pirate, organized on the island of Barataria with his brother Pierre, was instrumental in the American victory at New Orleans
Figure: Governor William Claiborne put out an order for this pirate's capture; Commodore Daniel Patterson later captured many of his supporters and ships, though he was eventually pardoned
Figure: this British major-general died leading troops at the Battle of New Orleans alongside Admiral Alexander Cochrane
Event: Black regiments fought on both sides of the Battle of New Orleans
Figure: a drummer with the 7th Army Regiment at New Orleans, assisted in battle by Major D'Aquin's Battalion of Free Men of Color
Work: set to the tune of "Unfortunate Miss Bailey," celebrates the "Hunters of Kentucky" ("half a horse" and "half an alligator") defending the Chalmette Plantation
Event: the Hartford Convention proposed requiring a two-thirds majority in Congress to admit new states or declare war
Figure: financed the war largely through deficit spending as Treasury Secretary
Figure: gave up commerce at the war's onset, becoming a farmer and writing pacifist letters
Event: an American expedition struggled to cross the Black Swamp after a militia mutinied over clothing rations
Treaty: formally ended the War of 1812, though the Battle of New Orleans was fought before news of it arrived
Treaty: limited naval presence on Lake Champlain and the Great Lakes, signed after the War of 1812's conclusion
Event: Black Refugees and Black Loyalists were sent to Nova Scotia after fighting for the British in this war
Treaty: negotiated by Potawatomi leaders Billy Caldwell and Alexander Robinson in the war's aftermath
Event: the wartime performance of Charles de Salaberry's Voltigeurs was mythologized by the Chateau Clique, boosting its postwar prominence alongside the Family Compact
